elements.sent-2411-001: Despite the impressive performance of large multimodal models (LMMs) in high-level visual tasks, their capacity for image quality assessment (IQA) remains limited.
elements.sent-2411-002: One main reason is that LMMs are primarily trained for high-level tasks (e.g., image captioning), emphasizing unified image semantics extraction under varied quality.
elements.sent-2411-003: Such semantic-aware yet quality-insensitive perception bias inevitably leads to a heavy reliance on image semantics when those LMMs are forced for quality rating.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, instead of retraining or tuning an LMM costly, we propose a training-free debiasing framework, in which the image quality prediction is rectified by mitigating the bias caused by image semantics.
elements.sent-2411-005: Specifically, we first explore several semantic-preserving distortions that can significantly degrade image quality while maintaining identifiable semantics.
elements.sent-2411-006: By applying these specific distortions to the query/test images, we ensure that the degraded images are recognized as poor quality while their semantics remain.
elements.sent-2411-007: During quality inference, both a query image and its corresponding degraded version are fed to the LMM along with a prompt indicating that the query image quality should be inferred under the condition that the degraded one is deemed poor quality.
elements.sent-2411-008: This prior condition effectively aligns the LMM’s quality perception, as all degraded images are consistently rated as poor quality, regardless of their semantic difference.
elements.sent-2411-009: Finally, the quality scores of the query image inferred under different prior conditions (degraded versions) are aggregated using a conditional probability model.
elements.sent-2411-010: Extensive experiments on various IQA datasets show that our debiasing framework could consistently enhance the LMM performance and the code will be publicly available.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{sec/Imgs/intro.jpg} \caption{Illustration of perception bias in Large Multimodal Model (LMM) during quality assessment. Image quality ratings from the LMM (mPLUG-Owl3~\cite{ye2024mplugo3}) were obtained using the Q-Bench testing framework~\cite{wu2023qbench}. The LMM consistently assigns higher quality ratings to images in the second row compared to the first, despite both sets exhibiting similar quality distributions as measured by Mean Opinion Scores (MOSs). This discrepancy suggests that the LMM relies more on image semantics than on low-level image clues for quality assessment. Zoom in for enhanced clarity.} \label{fig:intro}
elements.sent-2411-011: Image Quality Assessment (IQA) models aim to measure image quality in alignment with human perception, playing a fundamental role across various computer vision tasks \cite{wang2006modern .}
elements.sent-2411-012: According to the availability of reference information, the IQA models can be classified into Full-Reference IQA (FR-IQA), Reduced-Reference IQA (RR-IQA), and No-Reference IQA (NR-IQA) \cite{zhai2020perceptual .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Among these, NR-IQA presents particular values as the image quality can be predicted without a reference requirement.
elements.sent-2411-014: In the past decades, significant progress has been witnessed in NR-IQA, including traditional hand-crafted feature-based models \cite{mittal2012making, gu2013no, min2017unified, min2017blind and deep learning-based models \cite{kang2014convolutional, zhang2018blind, zhu2020metaiqa, gao2022image, sun2023blind .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Despite the advancement, current models still grapple with limited generalization capabilities on unseen scenes and distortions due to the significant distribution shifts between training and test sets.
elements.sent-2411-016: Recently, the emergence of Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) \cite{openai_gpt4v_2023 has demonstrated impressive generalization abilities across various vision-language tasks, such as classification \cite{wang2023large , image captioning \cite{yu2023mm , and visual question answering \cite{shang2024traveler .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, the focus on high-level visual tasks usually limits their effectiveness in low-level tasks, such as IQA \cite{wu2023qbench, sun2024explore .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Although pioneering researchers have attempted to fine-tune or retrain LMMs for improved IQA accuracy \cite{wu2023align,chen2024q,zhu20242afc , the laborious dataset construction and costly model training usually render this approach inefficient.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Moreover, tuning LMMs specifically for IQA also introduces the risk of catastrophic forgetting \cite{luo2023empirical , compromising the retention of general knowledge and ultimately degrading the models' capability on other tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-020: A powerful strategy to both retain the LMM’s strengths across tasks and enhance its IQA performance is to unlock its vast general knowledge through well-crafted prompts, encouraging the LMM to respond accurately to quality rating requests.
elements.sent-2411-021: However, despite this potential, LMMs—driven by training objectives that emphasize semantic extraction over quality—usually default to interpreting image quality heavily relying on image semantics.
elements.sent-2411-022: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:intro , two sets of distorted images with different semantics are presented to an LMM for quality rating.}
elements.sent-2411-023: The results indicate that the LMM consistently prefers the quality of images with the second set of semantics, despite both sets having similar quality (Mean Opinion Score (MOS)) distributions.
elements.sent-2411-024: The case reveals that the LMM intrinsically bases its quality rating on image semantics rather than on quality-related clues (\eg, noise or blur).
elements.sent-2411-025: Motivated by this observation, we introduce an innovative, training-free approach to mitigate the perception bias inherent in LMMs.
elements.sent-2411-026: Our enhancement strategy consists of two main steps: 1) bias exposure, and 2) bias mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-027: In the bias exposure step, we assume that the same bias exists consistently across images sharing the same semantics.
elements.sent-2411-028: Based on this assumption, we can expose the perception bias of a query/test image by measuring how much the LMM resists labeling it as high quality when its quality is severely degraded but the semantics are preserved.
elements.sent-2411-029: To achieve this, we explore several specific distortions that drastically corrupt the image quality while preserving its semantics to some extent.
elements.sent-2411-030: For a query image, we then impose these distortions on the query image and obtain its degraded versions.
elements.sent-2411-031: Herein, those degraded images should be deemed as poor quality.
elements.sent-2411-032: However, the LMM may not always agree with the fact and the disagreement leads to the bias exposure.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the bias mitigation step, we address the exposed bias using an instructive conditional prompt.
elements.sent-2411-034: Specifically, during inference, we provide both the query image and its degraded version to the LMM, along with a prompt indicating that the quality of the query image should be assessed under the condition that the degraded counterpart is rated as poor quality.
elements.sent-2411-035: By forcing the LMM to rate the quality of the degraded images appropriately, the bias mitigation in turn refines the LMM’s quality prediction for the query image.
elements.sent-2411-036: Finally, the quality predictions under different distortions are aggregated through a conditional probability model, further improving the prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-037: Before delving into detail, we highlight our main contributions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-038: Investigation of Perception Bias in LMM for IQA.
elements.sent-2411-039: We explore the perception bias inherent in LMM when used for IQA.
elements.sent-2411-040: Our training-free approach, which requires no task-specific fine-tuning, highlights a new pathway for leveraging pre-trained LMM on unseen tasks.
elements.sent-2411-042: We introduce a simple yet effective conditional prompt to mitigate the semantic bias in quality assessment.
elements.sent-2411-043: The prompt encourages the LMM to rate the image quality by aligning the quality prediction of synthetically degraded images, effectively reducing the bias caused by the semantics varies.
elements.sent-2411-044: Additionally, a confidence-based quality aggregation model is designed, further enhancing the prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-045: Comprehensive Evaluation on Diverse Datasets and Distortions.
elements.sent-2411-046: We extensively evaluated our method on both natural and AI-generated images and the superior performance underscores the high effectiveness of our bias mitigation strategy.
elements.sent-2411-047: In addition, consistent improvements across multiple LMMs also demonstrate the strong generalization of our method, highlighting its potential to successfully extend to future LMMs.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{sec/Imgs/frame.png} \caption{The framework of our perception bias mitigation scheme. It mainly consists of two components: 1) Bias Exposure: Specific distortions are imposed on the query image to significantly degrade the query image quality while preserving its semantics. The disagreement that the LMM rates those distorted images as poor quality exposes the perception bias inherent in the LMM. 2) Bias Mitigation: Dedicated prompts are defined to mitigate the bias by forcing that the quality of the query image should be assessed under the condition that its degraded counterpart is rated as poor quality. The final quality is then estimated by a semantic similarity based aggregation.} \label{fig:architecture}
elements.sent-2411-048: Traditional NR-IQA methods mainly rely on handcrafted features to model natural scene statistics (NSS) and estimate image quality by assessing the destruction level of the NSS.
elements.sent-2411-049: For example, Mean-Subtracted Contrast-Normalized (MSCN) coefficients were explored to model NSS as a unified distribution, enabling quality estimation through distributional discrepancy analysis \cite{mittal2012making .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Such NSS descriptors were also widely investigated in the wavelet domain \cite{moorthy2010two,tang2011learning , gradient domain \cite{xue2014blind as well as the discrete cosine transform (DCT) domain \cite{saad2012blind .}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Drawing inspiration from the free-energy brain theory that the human visual system (HVS) strives to minimize uncertainty through an internal generative model \cite{friston2006free, friston2010free , psychovisual quality models were proposed in \cite{gu2013no, gu2014using, zhai2011psychovisual .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Tough valuable insights from these studies, the inherent complexity of HVS continues to challenge feature design, prompting a shift toward data-driven feature learning.
elements.sent-2411-053: Kang \etal proposed the first convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for IQA and extended this work into a multi-task learning framework \cite{kang2014convolutional,kang2015simultaneous .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Inspired by the success, more advanced networks have emerged including DIQaM \cite{bosse2017deep , RankIQA \cite{liu2017rankiqa , FPR \cite{chen2022no , and GraphIQA \cite{sun2022graphiqa .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: However, limited data scales and learning strategies still restrict their generalization across different distributions.
elements.sent-2411-056: To account for this, some works leverage general knowledge learned from other tasks for IQA, such as image classification \cite{you2021transformer , image restoration \cite{lin2018hallucinated , and image-language correspondence \cite{radford2021learning, wang2023exploring .}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Other attempts have been dedicated to the new learning paradigms, including meat learning \cite{zhu2020metaiqa , continual learning \cite{zhang2024task , curriculum learning \cite{wang2023hierarchical , and domain adaptation/generalization \cite{chen2021no, chen2021learning .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Despite the impressive progress, enhancing generalization in NR-IQA remains a significant challenge.. LMM-based IQA.
elements.sent-2411-059: Recently, high-performance LMMs such as ChatGPT-4v \cite{openai_gpt4v_2023 , LLaVA \cite{liu2024improved,liu2024visual , and mPLUG-Owl3 \cite{ye2024mplugo3 have exhibited strong zero-shot transfer capabilities across various vision tasks, suggesting their potential applicability in the IQA task.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Early efforts, such as Q-Bench \cite{wu2023qbench and DepictQA \cite{you2024descriptive , established comprehensive benchmarks to evaluate LMMs in low-level visual perception.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: In \cite{zhu20242afc , Zhu \etal examined the IQA performance of LMMs under the two-alternative forced choice (2AFC) prompting.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Instead of score regression, Q-Align \cite{wu2023align enabled LMMs to rate images at text-defined rating levels, excelling in both quality and aesthetic assessment tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-063: By utilizing pre-trained LMMs and extensive datasets, recent studies have continued to improve IQA outcomes \cite{wu2024qinstruct, wu2024towards, zhu2024adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Although fine-tuning LMMs for IQA yields strong performance, it also poses the risk of catastrophic forgetting \cite{luo2023empirical problems, leading to the degraded generalization capability on other tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-065: In light of these limitations, training-free approaches offer a more promising alternative to fully harness the extensive knowledge in LMMs for IQA.
elements.sent-2411-066: However, this strategy remains relatively underexplored.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{sec/Imgs/dist.png} \caption{Illustration of the four distortion types which could degrade image quality significantly while largely preserving its semantics.} \label{fig:dist}
elements.sent-2411-067: Given a query image $x$, the typical prompt for the LMM in IQA is exemplified as follows: \begin{spacing {1.2 \fontfamily{cmtt}\selectfont { \#User: \textcolor{BrickRed {{Rate the quality of the image.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Good or poor? {{(Question) {[IMAGE\_TOKEN] {(Image) \ \#Assistant: \textcolor{BrickRed {{The quality of the image is [SCORE\_TOKEN].}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: However, the semantic bias inherent in the LMM usually results in unreliable quality estimation, as the inference heavily relies on image semantics.
elements.sent-2411-070: To account for this, we adopt a conditional probability model to mitigate the bias, which can be formulated as follows, \begin{equation}p(y \mid x)=\mathrm{E}_{x^{\prime} \mid x} p\left(y \mid x, x^{\prime}\right) p\left(x^{\prime} \mid x\right), \label{eqn:pyxx}\end{equation} where $x^{\prime}$ is a ``conditional image" of $x$, whose quality has been severely degraded while retaining similar semantics to $x$.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}p(y \mid x)=E_{x^{\prime} \mid x} p\left(y \mid x, x^{\prime}\right) p\left(x^{\prime} \mid x\right), \label{eqn:pyxx}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-071: During inference, both the conditional image and the query image are fed to the LMM with a prompt instructing the LMM to rate the quality of the query image, under the condition that the conditional image is considered of poor quality.
elements.sent-2411-072: Our design philosophy is to guide the LMM toward confidently and accurately classifying the degraded images as poor quality, reducing its high reliance on image semantics in quality inference.
elements.sent-2411-073: This bias mitigation can, in turn, be propagated to the query image quality inference, assuming that the bias is consistently present in images with similar semantics but varying distortions.
elements.sent-2411-074: Guided by the model constructed in Eqn.~(\ref{eqn:pyxx ), we design our framework mainly comprises two components: 1) Bias Exposure.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Specific distortions that significantly degrade image quality while preserving semantics are explored and imposed on the query image to construct different conditional images $p\left(x^{\prime \mid x\right)$.. 2) Bias Mitigation.}
elements.sent-2411-076: A dedicated prompt is designed to estimate $p\left(y \mid x, x^{\prime}\right)$ across different conditional images and obtain the final quality by Eqn.~(\ref{eqn:pyxx ).}
elements.sent-2411-077: Our framework is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:architecture and each component is detailed as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Given a query image, we examine four typical distortions—zoom blur, spatter noise, saturation enlargement, and fog corruption—to effectively degrade the image quality while preserving its semantic content.. Specifically: Zoom Blur.
elements.sent-2411-079: The zoom blur distortion usually occurs when a camera moves toward an object rapidly, which can be simulated by \begin{equation}x^{\prime}_{\text {1}}(u, v)=\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n x_{z_i}(u, v), \label{eqn:zoom}\end{equation} where $x_{z_i}$ means the zoom result of the query image $x$ by a factor $z_i$ and a total of $n$ factors are adopted.
elements.sent-2411-080: We use spatter noise to mimic the distortion caused by an unclean camera lens due to bad weather conditions such as rain, mud, or dust, which can be generated by \begin{equation}x_2^{\prime}(u, v)=x(u, v) \cdot(1-M(u, v))+C(u, v) \cdot M(u, v),\end{equation} where $M(u, v)$ is the spatter mask indicating regions that are affected or unaffected, and $C(u, v)$ represents the specific color distribution adapted for different splatter types.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}x_2^{\prime}(u, v)=x(u, v) \cdot(1-M(u, v))+C(u, v) \cdot M(u, v),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-081: Herein, we use the implementation in \cite{hendrycks2019robustness to generate \( M(u, v) \) and \( C(u, v) \).. Saturation Enlargement.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The saturation distortion modifies the saturation channel of an image in the HSV color space based on the severity parameter $c_0$, which can be defined by \begin{equation}x^{\prime}_{\text {3}} = f_{hsv2rgb} (x_h, x^{\prime}_s, x_v),\end{equation} with \begin{equation}x^{\prime}_s = \operatorname{clip}\left(x_s \cdot c, 0,1\right), \label{eqn:sat}\end{equation} where $x_h, x_s$, and $x_v$ represent the hue, saturation, and value components of $x$ in HSV space, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-083: The function $f_{hsv2rgb (\cdot)$ converts the image from HSV to RGB space, while $\operatorname{clip (\cdot)$ ensures that the distorted results are clipped to a valid range.. Fog Corruption.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: We simulate a foggy environment by applying a haze effect to the query image as follows: \begin{equation}x^{\prime}_{\text {4}} = \operatorname{clip}\left(x +k \cdot x^{F}, 0, 1 \right), \label{eqn:fog}\end{equation} where the $ x^{F}$ is the fog pattern generated by a diamond-square algorithm \cite{fournier1982computer,hendrycks2019robustness based on $x$.}
elements.sent-2411-085: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:dist , the four types of distortion degrade the image quality significantly while the semantics are still recognizable, leading to an expected bia exposure when they are not deemed poor quality by the LMM.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Based on the generated conditional images for each query image, we then input the query image ($x$) and one of its counterparts ($x^{\prime _i$) into the LMM, using a specific prompt to propagate the bias mitigation effect from the conditional image to the query image.}
elements.sent-2411-087: How about the visual quality of the second image? Good or poor? {{(Question) [IMAGE\_TOKEN1, IMAGE\_TOKEN2].{(Image1, Image2) \#Assistant: \textcolor{BrickRed { The quality of the image is [SCORE\_TOKEN].}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Finally, we aggregate the quality estimation across the four distortion types: \begin{equation}p(y \mid x)=\sum_{i=1}^{4} p\left(y \mid x, x^{\prime}_i\right) p\left({x_i}^{\prime} \mid x\right), \label{eqn:aggr}\end{equation} where \( p\left(x^{\prime _i \mid x\right) \) is the probability that the distorted image is adopted as the condition.}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}p(y \mid x)=\sum_{i=1}^{4} p\left(y \mid x, x^{\prime}_i\right) p\left({x_i}^{\prime} \mid x\right), \label{eqn:aggr}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-089: We leverage the semantic similarity between \( x \) and \( x^{\prime \) to estimate this probability, based on the assumption that the more semantic information maintained, the more confidently the image can be considered as a condition.}
elements.sent-2411-090: We achieve the semantic similarity estimation by feeding another prompt to the LMM as follows, \begin{spacing {1.2 \fontfamily{cmtt}\selectfont{ \#User: \textcolor{BrickRed {Do these two images describe the same object? Yes or no? {\fontfamily{cmtt}\selectfont {(Question) \#Assistant: \textcolor{BrickRed {[SCORE\_TOKEN].}}}}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{sec/Imgs/exp.jpg} \caption{Visualization of image quality prediction results. In each subfigure, the top-left label shows numbers in \textcolor{green}{green}, \textcolor{blue}{blue}, and \textcolor{red}{red}, representing the \textcolor{green}{MOS}, the \textcolor{blue}{LMM prediction result with the prompt in Q-Bench}, and \textcolor{red}{Our result}, respectively.} \label{fig:lmmimg}
elements.sent-2411-091: We evaluate our method on six publicly available datasets: LIVE Challenge~\cite{ghadiyaram2015massive , KonIQ-10k~\cite{koniq10k , AGIQA-3k~\cite{10262331 , KADID-10k~\cite{kadid10k , SPAQ~\cite{fang2020cvpr and SIQAD~\cite{yang2015perceptual .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The KonIQ-10k, SPAQ, and LIVE Challenge datasets are in-the-wild image collections, featuring authentic distortions.
elements.sent-2411-093: KonIQ-10k and SPAQ datasets each contain over 10,000 images and the SPAQ dataset is specifically designed to assess the quality of images captured by smartphones.
elements.sent-2411-094: The KADID-10k dataset comprises 10,125 images with various systematic distortions.
elements.sent-2411-095: The AGIQA-3k dataset includes 2,900 images focused on AI-generated image quality assessment.
elements.sent-2411-096: The SIQAD dataset is constructed for screen content image (SCI) quality assessment and a total of 980 distorted SCIs are involved.. Comparison Methods.
elements.sent-2411-097: We denote our method as ``Q-Debias" and compare its performance against both training-free (opinion-unaware) and training-based methods across multiple datasets.
elements.sent-2411-098: The training-free methods include BLINDS-II~\cite{moorthy2010two , QAC~\cite{xue2013learning , BRISQUE~\cite{mittal2012no , NIQE~\cite{mittal2012making , ILNIQE~\cite{zhang2015feature , NPQI~\cite{liu2020blind , ContentSep~\cite{babu2023no , CLIP-IQA~\cite{wang2023exploring , MDFS~\cite{ni2024opinion , and Q-Bench~\cite{wu2023qbench .}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: In particular, Q-Bench refers to using the same LMM (mPLUG-Owl3) with our method, while applying the query prompt from Q-Bench.
elements.sent-2411-100: For the training-based methods, we adopt models trained on the large-scale KonIQ-10k dataset for comparison, including ARNIQA~\cite{agnolucci2024arniqa , TReS~\cite{golestaneh2021no , and MUSIQ~\cite{ke2021musiq .}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: We list their performance in cross-dataset settings to verify their generalization capability.
elements.sent-2411-102: The Pearson Linear Correlation Coefficient (PLCC) and the Spearman Rank-Order Correlation Coefficient (SRCC) are used as metrics to assess the linearity and monotonicity of our quality predictions.. Implementation Details.
elements.sent-2411-103: We select mPLUG-Owl3 as our LMM due to its superior performance on image processing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-104: We set \( z_i \in \{1.00, 1.01, 1.02, \dots, 1.10\} \), \( n = 11 \) in Eqn.~(\ref{eqn:zoom ), and \( c = 2.0 \) in Eqn.~(\ref{eqn:sat ).}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Our code is implemented in PyTorch and runs on an A6000 GPU.

elements.sent-2411-107: As shown in Table~\ref{tab1 , compared with existing training-free methods, our model Q-Debias consistently achieves the best performance across the first five IQA datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-108: In particular, most hand-crafted feature-based models (\eg, NIQE) experience a significant performance drop on the KADID-10k dataset due to the diverse distortion types involved.
elements.sent-2411-109: However, our method still outperforms these models by a large margin, demonstrating its high effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-110: Compared to the vanilla prompt used in Q-Bench, our method—while utilizing the same base LMM (mPLUG-Owl3)—achieves performance gains across all five datasets.
elements.sent-2411-111: Notably, both Q-Bench and our method do not achieve promising results on the SIQAD dataset.
elements.sent-2411-112: The reason may lie in that LMMs are primarily trained on natural images, while the SCIs in the SIQAD dataset have been less extensively explored.
elements.sent-2411-113: From our experiments, we observe that not all distortion types for conditional image generation are effective for SIQAD.
elements.sent-2411-114: However, the introduction of single spatter noise demonstrates a positive effect on the SIQAD dataset, suggesting that our conditional prompt strategy can still be effective when specific distortions are carefully explored.
elements.sent-2411-115: In comparison to training-free methods, training-based models generally deliver superior results, benefiting from the quality assessment knowledge learned from large-scale datasets.
elements.sent-2411-116: However, due to the fact that KonIQ-10k only contains authentic distortions, these models often underperform on unseen distortions when tested on datasets involving unseen distortions (e.g., TReS: 0.771 on LIVE Challenge vs 0.468 on KADID-10k, MUSIQ: 0.788 on LIVE Challenge vs 0.630 on AGIQA-3k), highlighting the overfitting dilemma during training.
elements.sent-2411-117: In contrast, our approach improves the LMM in a training-free manner, providing superior generalization capability across authentic, systemic, and AI-generated distortions.. Visualization.
elements.sent-2411-118: To verify the effectiveness of our method, we visualize our predicted results alongside the LMM predictions using the Q-Bench prompt on the KADID-10k dataset (first two rows) and the AIGC-3k dataset (second two rows).
elements.sent-2411-119: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:lmmimg , we can observe that: 1) Despite comparable distortions and closely aligned MOS distributions in the first two rows, the LMM without our debiasing enhancement consistently assigns higher quality ratings to images in the first row over the second.}
elements.sent-2411-120: This observation underscores the model's high reliance on semantic content rather than low-level clues for quality assessment, revealing the presence of perceptual bias.
elements.sent-2411-121: In contrast, our debias strategy could effectively mitigate such bias, resulting in predictions that are more consistent with human ratings (\ie, MOSs).
elements.sent-2411-122: In our method, we adopted the multimodal model mPLUG-Owl3 as our foundation model.
elements.sent-2411-123: To demonstrate the generalization capability of our enhancement strategy, we further validate it on another two LMMs inducing: BakLLaVA~\cite{liu2024improved , and Qwen-VL~\cite{bai2023qwen .}}
elements.sent-2411-124: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:lmm , we could observe a consistent average performance gains can be achieved.}
elements.sent-2411-125: Notably, the improvements observed on the LIVE Challenge and AGIQA-3k datasets suggest that semantic bias is widespread across diverse content types and such bias can be mitigated by our method efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-126: The promising generalization capability highlights the transformative potential of our bias-mitigation strategy and opens up exciting new avenues for developing training-free enhancement methods to fully harness the potential of LMMs for unseen tasks.

elements.sent-2411-127: Three main components are designed in our debias scheme: 1) the conditional images, 2) the instructive prompt, and 3) the aggregation scheme.
elements.sent-2411-128: To verify the effectiveness of each component, we ablate each component from our scheme and verify their effectiveness as follows.
elements.sent-2411-130: As shown in Table~\ref{tab3 , we compare our method with eight types of variants: (1) no conditional images, (2)-(5) each variant with a single image, distorted by one of the four distortion types individually, (6) two images with the top two performing distortion types, (7) three images with the top three performing distortion types, and (8) the conditional images with semantics that do not match the query image.}
elements.sent-2411-131: To establish a representative baseline, we first select images from open-source datasets that do not share semantics with those in the LIVE Challenge, SIQAD, and AGIQA-3k datasets.
elements.sent-2411-132: Four specific distortions are then applied to these selected images to create an additional conditional image set.
elements.sent-2411-133: Finally, we randomly select four low-quality images from this set as the conditional images.
elements.sent-2411-134: The results reveal that: 1) Each distortion type contributes positively to our final image quality prediction, with the Saturate distortion achieving the highest performance on the natural images and generated images (LIVE Challenge and AGIQA-3k datasets).
elements.sent-2411-135: This reveals that bias is highly semantic-specific; using conditional images with matching semantics to the query image enables remarkably accurate bias estimation and consistently enhances quality prediction.
elements.sent-2411-137: In our method, the prompt serves an instructive role for the LMM, facilitating the propagation of bias mitigation from the conditional images to the query image.
elements.sent-2411-138: To verify its effectiveness, we compare our method against three prompt variants: (T1) Prompt Replacement: The entire prompt is replaced with ``{\fontfamily{cmtt {\selectfont{\textcolor{BrickRed {Rate the quality of the second image.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Good or poor? ” (T2) Bias Exposure Ablation: The phrase ``The visual quality of the first image is poor” is removed from our prompt to examine the role of bias exposure, leading to the second prompt: ``{\fontfamily{cmtt {\selectfont{\textcolor{BrickRed {How about the visual quality of the second image? Good or poor? " (T3) Bias Mitigation Propagation Ablation: We delete the ``How about" from the prompt to assess the impact of bias mitigation propagation to the query image, resulting in the third prompt: ``{\fontfamily{cmtt {\selectfont{\textcolor{BrickRed {The visual quality of the first image is poor.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: Rate the visual quality of the second image.
elements.sent-2411-141: Good or poor? " As shown in Table~\ref{tab4 , the results reveal that: 1) Without our instructive prompt, even with the conditional images provided, bias cannot be effectively mitigated.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Without this phrase, the propagation of bias mitigation from the conditional images to the query image weakens noticeably.
elements.sent-2411-143: The best results are achieved when the full prompt is included, demonstrating the necessity of each instruction in our prompt.
elements.sent-2411-145: To aggregate the quality scores derived from different conditional images, we introduce a semantic similarity aggregation strategy.
elements.sent-2411-146: To assess its effectiveness, we compare our method with four alternative schemes: 1) Average Aggregation: Each of the four quality scores is assigned an equal weight during aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-147: These quality similarity scores are then treated as weights for the aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-148: The results are presented in Table~\ref{tab5 , which reveal that: 1) The Average scheme results in a noticeable performance drop, suggesting that uniform weighting fails to account for the bias variations across different types of distortions.}
elements.sent-2411-149: The possible reason may lie that a higher quality similarity score does not always correspond to a higher semantic recognition for the LMM, due to perceptual discrepancy between the LMM and the human visual system.
elements.sent-2411-150: In comparison, our semantic similarity aggregation scheme delivers the best performance across datasets containing synthetic, authentic, and generative distortions, demonstrating its superior generalization on diverse image distortions.
elements.sent-2411-151: In this paper, we propose a training-free scheme to enhance the LMM in the IQA task.
elements.sent-2411-152: In particular, the perception bias that the LMM infers image quality highly relies on image semantics is mitigated by introducing conditional images in the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-153: These conditional images share the similar semantics as the query image but experience degraded quality.
elements.sent-2411-154: By instructing the LMM to align its quality ratings on those conditional images, the alignment in turn forces the LMM to rectify their judgment on the query image.
elements.sent-2411-155: Experimental results on images with different distortions verify the effectiveness of our method, and the generalization capability of our scheme across other LMMs highlights the potential for advanced prompt designs to fully leverage LMM knowledge for unseen tasks.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-156: After receiving paper reviews, authors may optionally submit a rebuttal to address the reviewers' comments, which will be limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-157: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-158: The author rebuttal is optional and, following similar guidelines to previous conferences, is meant to provide you with an opportunity to rebut factual errors or to supply additional information requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-159: It is NOT intended to add new contributions (theorems, algorithms, experiments) that were absent in the original submission and NOT specifically requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-160: You may optionally add a figure, graph, or proof to your rebuttal to better illustrate your answer to the reviewers' comments.
elements.sent-2411-161: Per a passed 2018 PAMI-TC motion, reviewers should refrain from requesting significant additional experiments for the rebuttal or penalize for lack of additional experiments.
elements.sent-2411-162: Authors should refrain from including new experimental results in the rebuttal, especially when not specifically requested to do so by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-163: Authors may include figures with illustrations or comparison tables of results reported in the submission/supplemental material or in other papers.
elements.sent-2411-164: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-165: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-166: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-167: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-168: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-169: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-170: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-171: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-172: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-173: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-174: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-176: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-177: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-178: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-179: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-180: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-181: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in \cref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-182: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-183: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-184: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.
elements.figure-2411-010: \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{0.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{Example of caption. It is set in Roman so that mathematics (always set in Roman: $B \sin A = A \sin B$) may be included without an ugly clash.} \label{fig:onecol}
elements.sent-2411-185: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about \cref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-188: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
elements.sent-2411-189: Resize fonts in figures to match the font in the body text, and choose line widths which render effectively in print.
elements.sent-2411-190: Readers (and reviewers), even of an electronic copy, may choose to print your response in order to read it.
elements.sent-2411-191: You cannot insist that they do otherwise, and therefore must not assume that they can zoom in to see tiny details on a graphic.
elements.sent-2411-192: When placing figures in \LaTeX, it is almost always best to use \verb+\includegraphics+, and to specify the figure width as a multiple of the line width as in the example below { .}
